claims.claim_text: In 2017, a GitLab engineer accidentally deleted a production database, and GitLab's postmortem describes a complex recovery that depended on replicas and backups.
claims.span_hint: section: RAM: Your Counter Space; paragraph after 'When RAM fills up'
claims.rationale: This is a named real outage and should point to the primary postmortem.

claims.claim_text: Thirty browser tabs alone can eat 4-6 GB of RAM.
claims.span_hint: section: RAM: Your Counter Space; pause-and-predict callout
claims.suggested_rewrite: If you open a browser with many tabs plus other heavy apps, a typical laptop can become painfully slow as the OS starts paging memory to disk.
claims.rationale: Per-tab browser memory use varies too much for a precise number here, but the paging/slowness lesson is valid.

claims.claim_text: Windows is the most common desktop OS worldwide at 60.8% in March 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System; three main operating systems list
claims.rationale: This is a dated market-share statistic and the cited dashboard shows that figure for March 2026.

claims.claim_text: Linux is the default environment you'll see in most Kubernetes tutorials and many production clusters.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System; paragraph before the stop-and-think callout
claims.suggested_rewrite: In Kubernetes learning materials and labs, you'll usually see Linux first, even though Kubernetes also supports Windows worker nodes.
claims.rationale: The broad ecosystem-usage claim is directionally true but not tightly sourced from one allowlisted page.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes worker nodes can run either Linux or Microsoft Windows, while the control plane stays on Linux.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System; sentence beginning 'The Kubernetes docs say'
claims.rationale: This is a versioned platform-capability statement directly covered by the Kubernetes documentation.

claims.claim_text: In the cloud, pricing depends on instance type, operating system, region, and purchase model.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Matters for Kubernetes; paragraph after the kitchen bullets
claims.rationale: AWS's EC2 estimate workflow explicitly varies cost by region, OS, instance choice, and payment option.

claims.claim_text: AWS listed a Linux t2.large instance with 8 GiB of RAM in us-east-1 at $0.0928/hour on April 15, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Matters for Kubernetes; sentence beginning 'For example, AWS listed'
claims.suggested_rewrite: For example, an 8 GiB cloud VM can cost on the order of cents per hour, depending on the region, operating system, and pricing model you choose.
claims.rationale: Exact dated cloud-price snapshots are unstable and the policy requires removing false precision rather than citing a moving pricing page.

claims.claim_text: A modern smartphone has more computing power than the computers NASA used to land on the moon in 1969; the Apollo Guidance Computer had 74 KB of memory; your phone has millions of times more.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; first bullet
claims.suggested_rewrite: A modern smartphone has vastly more memory and processing capacity than the computers used during Apollo; the Apollo Guidance Computer had only a few dozen kilobytes of onboard memory.
claims.rationale: The history point is real, but 'millions of times more' and 'more computing power' are too imprecise as written.

claims.claim_text: Early computers used magnetic-core memory, each ring stored one bit, and a full megabyte would require over 8 million rings.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; second bullet
claims.rationale: The cited page directly explains core-memory rings and one-bit-per-core storage.

claims.claim_text: SSDs have no moving parts and are generally faster, quieter, and more shock-resistant than HDDs.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; third bullet, first three sentences
claims.rationale: These are direct hardware-behavior claims and the SSD reference discusses them explicitly.

claims.claim_text: Drop a laptop with an HDD and you might lose data; drop one with an SSD and you probably won't.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; third bullet, final two sentences
claims.suggested_rewrite: Because SSDs have no moving parts, they are generally more resistant to physical shock than HDDs, but neither storage type is immune to data loss.
claims.rationale: The current wording overpromises outcomes after a drop that depend on many device-specific factors.

claims.claim_text: In 1947, engineers working on the Harvard Mark II found a moth stuck in a relay and logged it as an actual bug.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; fourth bullet, first two sentences
claims.rationale: This is a named historical computing anecdote and the Harvard Mark II page directly covers it.

claims.claim_text: The term debugging has been used in computer science ever since the Harvard Mark II moth incident.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know?; fourth bullet, final sentence
claims.suggested_rewrite: The incident became a famous early computing anecdote.
claims.rationale: The moth story is real, but the wording wrongly implies that the incident originated or singularly established the term.

claims.claim_text: A team might pay $400/month for a 32 GB RAM server when their application only uses 2 GB, and right-sizing saves thousands of dollars.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes; row 'Over-provisioning cloud servers'
claims.suggested_rewrite: In the cloud, an oversized server can easily cost far more than the application actually needs, and right-sizing can save a meaningful amount over time.
claims.rationale: The concrete dollar amounts are illustrative, but AWS does publish right-sizing guidance tied to underused CPU and memory.

further_reading.title: Memory paging
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful background for the RAM-versus-disk slowdown described in the module.

further_reading.title: Windows containers in Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the Linux control plane and Windows worker-node support that the module previews.

further_reading.title: Generating Amazon EC2 estimates
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows how instance type, RAM, OS, region, and payment options affect cloud cost.

notes: Skipped quiz/exercise prose, analogies, and general teaching flow. Existing inline links were treated as evidence candidates, but the dated exact AWS price was still marked unsalvageable per policy.
_schema_issues: claim[1]:hard_cite_class_cannot_soften:statistic/soften_to_illustration — must be supported, weak_anchor, or needs_allowlist_expansion
